source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Field-drive is a locomotion mechanism that is analogous to general relativistic warp-drive. In this mechanism, an active particle continuously climbs up the field-gradient generated by its own influence on the environment so that the particle can bootstrap itself into a constant non-zero velocity motion. Consider a field-drive in one-dimensional (the $\mathrm{O} x$ axis) environment, where the position of the particle at time $t$ is given by $X(t)$ and its instantaneous velocity follows from:
$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} t} X(t)=\left.\kappa \frac{\partial}{\partial x} R(x, t)\right|_{x=X(t)}
$$
in which $\kappa$ is called the guiding coefficient and $R(x, t)$ is the field-value in this space. Note that, the operation ... $\left.\right|_{x=X(t)}$ means you have to calculate the part in ... first, then replace $x$ with $X(t)$. For a biological example, the active particle can be a cell, the field can be the nutrient concentration, and the strategy of climbing up the gradient can be chemotaxis. The cell consumes the nutrient and also responses to the local nutrient concentration, biasing its movement toward the direction where the concentration increases the most. If the nutrient is not diffusive and always recovers locally (e.g. a surface secretion) to the value which we defined to be 0 , then its dynamics can usually be approximated by:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} R(x, t)=-\frac{1}{\tau} R(x, t)-\gamma \exp \left\{-\frac{[x-X(t)]^{2}}{2 \lambda^{2}}\right\}
$$
where $\tau$ is the timescale of recovery, $\gamma$ is the consumption, and $\lambda$ is the characteristic radius of influence. Before we inoculate the cell into the environment, $R=0$ everywhere at any time. What is the smallest guiding coefficient $\kappa$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ ) for field-drive to emerge, if the parameters are $\tau=50 \mathrm{~s}, \gamma=1 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, and $\lambda=10 \mu \mathrm{m}$.

It asks for the smallest guiding coefficient kappa for field-drive to emerge, which implies deriving or identifying a threshold condition before plugging in numbers
After obtaining the condition, the solver must substitute the provided parameters tau=50 s, gamma=1 s^-1, and lambda=10 um to get a numerical result
verifiability: The expected output is a specific numeric value, which is straightforward to compare against the provided answer_text
Multiple-choice options are provided, giving an additional simple consistency check

normalization_record.normalized_question_text:
Field-drive is a locomotion mechanism that is analogous to general relativistic warp-drive. In this mechanism, an active particle continuously climbs up the field-gradient generated by its own influence on the environment so that the particle can bootstrap itself into a constant non-zero velocity motion. Consider a field-drive in a one-dimensional (the $\mathrm{O}x$ axis) environment, where the position of the particle at time $t$ is given by $X(t)$ and its instantaneous velocity follows from:
$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} t} X(t)=\left.\kappa \frac{\partial}{\partial x} R(x, t)\right|_{x=X(t)}
$$
in which $\kappa$ is called the guiding coefficient and $R(x, t)$ is the field-value in this space. Note that the operation $\left.\right|_{x=X(t)}$ means you have to calculate the part in the expression first, then replace $x$ with $X(t)$. For a biological example, the active particle can be a cell, the field can be the nutrient concentration, and the strategy of climbing up the gradient can be chemotaxis. The cell consumes the nutrient and also responds to the local nutrient concentration, biasing its movement toward the direction where the concentration increases the most. If the nutrient is not diffusive and always recovers locally (e.g. a surface secretion) to the value defined to be 0, then its dynamics can usually be approximated by:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} R(x, t)=-\frac{1}{\tau} R(x, t)-\gamma \exp \left\{-\frac{[x-X(t)]^{2}}{2 \lambda^{2}}\right\}
$$
where $\tau$ is the timescale of recovery, $\gamma$ is the consumption, and $\lambda$ is the characteristic radius of influence. Before we inoculate the cell into the environment, $R=0$ everywhere at any time. What is the smallest guiding coefficient $\kappa$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$) for field-drive to emerge, if the parameters are $\tau=50 \, \mathrm{s}$, $\gamma=1 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $\lambda=10 \, \mu \mathrm{m}$?

where $\tau$ is the timescale of recovery, $\gamma$ is the consumption, and $\lambda$ is the characteristic radius of influence. Before we inoculate the cell into the environment, $R=0$ everywhere at any time. What is the smallest guiding coefficient $\kappa$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$) for field-drive to emerge, if the parameters are $\tau=50 \, \mathrm{s}$, $\gamma=1 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $\lambda=10 \, \mu \mathrm{m}$?

answer_slots.target_text: What is the smallest guiding coefficient $\kappa$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$) for field-drive to emerge, if the parameters are $\tau=50 \, \mathrm{s}$, $\gamma=1 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $\lambda=10 \, \mu \mathrm{m}$?

sentence_segments.text: Field-drive is a locomotion mechanism that is analogous to general relativistic warp-drive.

In this mechanism, an active particle continuously climbs up the field-gradient generated by its own influence on the environment so that the particle can bootstrap itself into a constant non-zero velocity motion.
Consider a field-drive in a one-dimensional (the $\mathrm{O}x$ axis) environment, where the position of the particle at time $t$ is given by $X(t)$ and its instantaneous velocity follows from:
\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} t} X(t)=\left.\kappa \frac{\partial}{\partial x} R(x, t)\right|_{x=X(t)}
in which $\kappa$ is called the guiding coefficient and $R(x, t)$ is the field-value in this space.
Note that the operation $\left.\right|_{x=X(t)}$ means you have to calculate the part in the expression first, then replace $x$ with $X(t)$.
For a biological example, the active particle can be a cell, the field can be the nutrient concentration, and the strategy of climbing up the gradient can be chemotaxis.
The cell consumes the nutrient and also responds to the local nutrient concentration, biasing its movement toward the direction where the concentration increases the most.
If the nutrient is not diffusive and always recovers locally (e.g.
a surface secretion) to the value defined to be 0, then its dynamics can usually be approximated by:
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} R(x, t)=-\frac{1}{\tau} R(x, t)-\gamma \exp \left\{-\frac{[x-X(t)]^{2}}{2 \lambda^{2}}\right\}
where $\tau$ is the timescale of recovery, $\gamma$ is the consumption, and $\lambda$ is the characteristic radius of influence.
Before we inoculate the cell into the environment, $R=0$ everywhere at any time.

What is the smallest guiding coefficient $\kappa$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$) for field-drive to emerge, if the parameters are $\tau=50 \, \mathrm{s}$, $\gamma=1 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $\lambda=10 \, \mu \mathrm{m}$?

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement fully specifies the physics setup, parameters, and target quantity, and the expected answer is a numeric value. The image is not needed to understand or answer the question.

risk_flags: metadata.extraction_notes indicates the original image_1 field was a PIL.Image object string rather than a path or URL

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is fully understandable and semantically complete from text alone, with a clear numeric target answer. The rewritten open version is usable, alignment is consistent, and solvability evidence shows no serious failure. The unused image marker is a non-fatal metadata issue, not a reason to reject a recoverable sample.

field_audit_records.before_value:
Field-drive is a locomotion mechanism that is analogous to general relativistic warp-drive. In this mechanism, an active particle continuously climbs up the field-gradient generated by its own influence on the environment so that the particle can bootstrap itself into a constant non-zero velocity motion. Consider a field-drive in one-dimensional (the $\mathrm{O} x$ axis) environment, where the position of the particle at time $t$ is given by $X(t)$ and its instantaneous velocity follows from:

$$
in which $\kappa$ is called the guiding coefficient and $R(x, t)$ is the field-value in this space. Note that, the operation ... $\left.\right|_{x=X(t)}$ means you have to calculate the part in ... first, then replace $x$ with $X(t)$. For a biological example, the active particle can be a cell, the field can be the nutrient concentration, and the strategy of climbing up the gradient can be chemotaxis. The cell consumes the nutrient and also responses to the local nutrient concentration, biasing its movement toward the direction where the concentration increases the most. If the nutrient is not diffusive and always recovers locally (e.g. a surface secretion) to the value which we defined to be 0 , then its dynamics can usually be approximated by:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} R(x, t)=-\frac{1}{\tau} R(x, t)-\gamma \exp \left\{-\frac{[x-X(t)]^{2}}{2 \lambda^{2}}\right\}
$$
where $\tau$ is the timescale of recovery, $\gamma$ is the consumption, and $\lambda$ is the characteristic radius of influence. Before we inoculate the cell into the environment, $R=0$ everywhere at any time. What is the smallest guiding coefficient $\kappa$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ ) for field-drive to emerge, if the parameters are $\tau=50 \mathrm{~s}, \gamma=1 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, and $\lambda=10 \mu \mathrm{m}$.
